problem_summary: Replacing the shattered front display assembly on a notch-era iPhone (X / XS / XR / 11 / 11 Pro) with a like-for-like screen.
safety_pre_check: Power off the iPhone fully and disconnect from any cable.
Wear safety glasses — shattered glass can flake off as you pry.
Disconnect the battery flex before doing any other internal work.
Work on a magnetic mat with bright overhead light to track tiny screws.

parts_summary.name: Replacement display assembly
parts_summary.specification: Must match the EXACT iPhone model — display assemblies are NOT interchangeable across X / XS / XR / 11 / 11 Pro

parts_summary.name: Perimeter adhesive strip
parts_summary.specification: Pre-cut adhesive matching the model — restores water resistance

tools_summary.name: Pentalobe P2 screwdriver
tools_summary.specification: 0.8 mm pentalobe (two screws on the bottom edge)

tools_summary.name: Tri-point Y000 screwdriver
tools_summary.specification: For the bracket over the display + battery flex connectors

tools_summary.name: Phillips #00 screwdriver
tools_summary.specification: For the earpiece / sensor cluster transfer

tools_summary.name: Suction cup
tools_summary.specification: Heavy-duty single suction cup with handle

tools_summary.name: Plastic opening pick
tools_summary.specification: Thin guitar-pick style for the perimeter adhesive

tools_summary.name: Heat pad or hair dryer
tools_summary.specification: Softens the perimeter adhesive — strongly recommended

scene_lock.subject: A notch-era iPhone (X-generation, black glossy front, aluminum frame, with severely shattered front glass) lying face-up on a clean white anti-static workbench.
scene_lock.environment: Clean lit workbench with a magnetic parts tray, white anti-static mat, and a bright overhead daylight LED panel.
scene_lock.hands_style: Adult hands wearing fitted blue nitrile gloves with anti-static fingertips.
scene_lock.style: Photorealistic instructional video style with crisp macro detail, even neutral lighting, real materials, no text overlay.
scene_lock.color_palette: Charcoal iPhone, blue gloves, white workbench, silver tools, neutral overhead daylight.
consistency_phrases: Keep the iPhone identity (color, notch-era body, glossy black front) consistent across all shots
Show the shattered front glass only until the screen is removed in step 3
Maintain the clean white anti-static workbench background
negative_cues: screwdriver bit mismatching the screw type

steps.title: Remove the bottom pentalobe screws
steps.description: Power the iPhone down fully, then remove the two pentalobe P2 screws on either side of the Lightning port at the bottom edge.
steps.subject_focus: The bottom edge of the iPhone and the two pentalobe screws on either side of the Lightning port.
steps.visual_prompt_start: Scene: Clean white anti-static workbench, bright overhead daylight LED. Subject: The notch-era iPhone with shattered front glass lying face-up. A blue-gloved hand holds a small P2 pentalobe screwdriver, the BIT placed precisely on the TINY SCREW in the METAL CHASSIS EDGE next to the Lightning port. The screwdriver tip is on the EXTERNAL screw on the metal edge — NOT on the front glass. Details: Photorealistic macro, the screw is visibly on the BOTTOM METAL EDGE of the phone, on either side of the Lightning port opening. Constraints: The screwdriver tip MUST be on the small chassis screw next to the Lightning port; it must NOT be anywhere on the cracked glass display.
steps.visual_prompt_end: Scene: Same workbench, same iPhone. Subject: Both bottom pentalobe screws are out of the chassis and resting on a magnetic tray next to the phone; the Lightning port now shows two empty screw holes. Details: Photorealistic macro, screws clearly visible on the tray.
steps.motion_prompt: Animate the gloved hand carefully unscrewing first the left, then the right pentalobe screw with controlled torque, lifting each free and placing it on the magnetic tray.
steps.narration: Power the iPhone down completely. Place it face-up on the bench, then use the pentalobe P2 driver to remove both bottom screws — one on each side of the Lightning port. Drop each screw onto the magnetic tray so you do not lose it.
steps.subtitle: Remove pentalobe screws.
steps.safety_note: Use the correct P2 pentalobe bit — a Phillips will strip the head.
steps.success_criteria: Both screws are on the magnetic tray.
steps.common_mistake: Stripping the screws by using a Phillips bit.

steps.title: Loosen the screen adhesive with heat
steps.description: Warm the perimeter of the screen for about a minute with a heat pad or hair dryer to soften the waterproof adhesive ring.
steps.subject_focus: The whole front of the iPhone, evenly warmed by the heat source.
steps.visual_prompt_start: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The shattered iPhone lies face-up. A gloved hand holds a small black heat pad or hair dryer just above the phone, centred over the screen. Details: Photorealistic close-up, very faint warmth haze visible above the phone, no flame, no smoke.
steps.visual_prompt_end: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The heat source is set down on the bench beside the phone; the iPhone front glass remains shattered but the adhesive perimeter is now warm and ready to release. Details: Photorealistic, no visible damage from heat, no melted plastic.
steps.motion_prompt: Animate the heat source being held over the screen with a slow even sweeping motion for about a minute, then set down beside the phone.
steps.narration: Warm the screen perimeter with a heat pad or hair dryer for about a minute, keeping it moving so you do not concentrate heat on one spot. This softens the rubbery adhesive ring that seals the screen to the chassis.
steps.subtitle: Warm the adhesive perimeter.
steps.safety_note: Do not overheat — the battery sits directly under the screen.
steps.success_criteria: The perimeter feels warm but not hot to a gloved fingertip.
steps.common_mistake: Pointing the heat at one spot long enough to burn the polariser or warp the frame.

steps.title: Lift the screen with suction + pick
steps.description: Stick a suction cup near the bottom edge above the home indicator, lift gently to create a sliver of a gap, slide a plastic opening pick into the seam and run it around the perimeter to break the adhesive.
steps.subject_focus: The suction cup pulling the cracked screen up and the pick sliding around the perimeter.
steps.visual_prompt_start: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: A black suction cup with a wooden handle is pressed firmly against the shattered front glass, just above the bottom edge. A BRIGHT BLUE plastic opening pick is held by a second gloved hand, ready to be inserted. Details: Photorealistic close-up, crack pattern still clearly visible, blue plastic pick clearly visible. Constraints: The opening pick MUST be BRIGHT BLUE plastic.
steps.visual_prompt_end: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The screen is lifted about 3 mm off the chassis on the bottom edge and a thin BRIGHT BLUE plastic opening pick (SAME blue pick as in the start frame) is slid into the gap along the right edge, breaking the adhesive seal. Details: Photorealistic, visible thin gap between screen and frame, no flex tearing, pick is BLUE. Constraints: The opening pick MUST be BRIGHT BLUE plastic, identical color to the start frame — DO NOT change the pick color to black.
steps.motion_prompt: Animate the suction cup pulling gently while the opening pick is inserted in the gap and slowly slid around the perimeter, breaking the adhesive seal section by section.
steps.narration: Stick the suction cup near the bottom edge of the screen and pull straight up gently — just enough to slip in the opening pick. Run the pick slowly around the perimeter to break the adhesive seal, keeping the screen attached to the chassis at the top right hinge area.
steps.subtitle: Suction + pick around perimeter.
steps.safety_note: Open the phone like a book hinged on the RIGHT — flex cables exit on that side.
steps.success_criteria: The screen lifts cleanly along three edges with the right edge still attached.
steps.common_mistake: Pulling the screen straight up and ripping the display flex cables.

steps.title: Disconnect the battery and display flex
steps.description: Open the screen like a book to about 90°, remove the small tri-point Y000 bracket over the connectors, then disconnect the battery flex first, followed by the display flex cables.
steps.subject_focus: The tri-point bracket over the battery + display flex connectors on the logic board.
steps.visual_prompt_start: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The iPhone is opened like a book hinged on the right at roughly 90 degrees, exposing the silver tri-point Y000 bracket covering the battery and display flex connectors. Details: Photorealistic macro, every screw and connector crisp.
steps.visual_prompt_end: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The Y000 bracket has been removed and set aside; both the battery flex and the display flex cables are disconnected from the logic board, the screen now fully separate from the chassis. Details: Photorealistic, connectors clearly visible on the logic board, flex cables resting flat.
steps.motion_prompt: Animate the tri-point screws being removed one by one and the bracket lifted away with a spudger; then the battery flex popped off the board first, then each display flex cable in turn.
steps.narration: Open the screen carefully to about ninety degrees, with the hinge on the right. Unscrew the tri-point Y000 bracket covering the battery and display connectors. Disconnect the battery flex first, then the display flex cables — always battery first to avoid a short.
steps.subtitle: Disconnect battery, then display.
steps.safety_note: ALWAYS disconnect the battery flex first to avoid shorting the logic board.
steps.success_criteria: Battery and display flex cables are off the board; the cracked screen separates cleanly from the chassis.
steps.common_mistake: Disconnecting the display flex before the battery flex and shorting a board trace.

steps.title: Transfer the earpiece and sensor cluster
steps.description: If the new display did not come pre-assembled, transfer the earpiece speaker and the Face ID / sensor flex assembly from the old cracked display onto the new one.
steps.subject_focus: The earpiece speaker and Face ID sensor cluster being moved from the old screen to the new one.
steps.visual_prompt_start: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The old cracked display lies face-down on a microfibre cloth; the metal earpiece bracket and Face ID sensor cluster on its top edge are exposed, held by Phillips #00 screws. Details: Photorealistic macro, screws clearly identifiable.
steps.visual_prompt_end: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The earpiece + sensor cluster has been moved to the matching cut-outs of the NEW REPLACEMENT DISPLAY (a SINGLE display, face-down). The old cracked display has been SET ASIDE OFF-FRAME. The same Phillips screws are reseated on the new display. Details: Photorealistic macro, EXACTLY ONE display in the frame, intact with no cracks, sensor cluster aligned. Constraints: ONLY ONE PHONE DISPLAY in the frame — the old cracked one is OFF-CAMERA; absolutely no duplicated phones.
steps.motion_prompt: Animate the Phillips screws being removed from the old display, the earpiece/sensor cluster lifted with a spudger and reseated on the new display, then the screws driven back in.
steps.narration: If your replacement screen did not include the Face ID cluster, transfer it now. Unscrew the small Phillips screws holding the metal earpiece bracket on the old display, lift the sensor cluster with a spudger, and seat it on the new display in the same orientation. The Face ID flex is paired to this phone — losing or damaging it disables Face ID permanently.
steps.subtitle: Move Face ID cluster.
steps.safety_note: NEVER swap the Face ID flex with one from another phone — it is paired to this device.
steps.success_criteria: The earpiece and sensor cluster sit flush on the new display with all screws reseated.
steps.common_mistake: Damaging the Face ID flex while transferring it — Face ID cannot be re-paired.

steps.title: Reconnect, test, and seal
steps.description: Reconnect display flex cables first, then the battery flex, reinstall the bracket, briefly power-on test the new screen, apply the new perimeter adhesive, close the screen, and reinstall the two pentalobe screws.
steps.subject_focus: Reconnecting flex cables, applying adhesive, closing the screen and screwing in.
steps.visual_prompt_start: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The new display is opened like a book on the right; gloved hands press its display flex cables back onto the logic board, then the battery flex. Details: Photorealistic macro, flex connectors clicking flush.
steps.visual_prompt_end: Scene: Same workbench. Subject: The iPhone is fully reassembled — new display closed, perimeter adhesive freshly applied, both pentalobe screws back in, and the screen shows the Apple boot logo. Details: Photorealistic, final hero shot, no cracks visible, phone looks brand new.
steps.motion_prompt: Animate flex cables clicking back in (display first, then battery), bracket reinstalled, brief power-on test showing the Apple logo, perimeter adhesive applied as a thin strip around the chassis, screen closed firmly, and pentalobe screws driven back in.
steps.narration: Reconnect the display flex cables first, then the battery flex, and reinstall the tri-point bracket. Briefly press the side button to confirm the new screen powers on and shows the Apple logo. Power down, apply the new perimeter adhesive strip around the chassis, close the screen evenly along the right hinge, and drive the two pentalobe screws back in.
steps.subtitle: Reconnect, test, seal.
steps.safety_note: Always reconnect display flex BEFORE battery flex; do the reverse only for disconnect.
steps.success_criteria: The new screen powers on cleanly and the phone is fully closed with both bottom screws.
steps.common_mistake: Forgetting the EMI bracket — without it the phone loses water resistance and may short.
